Marketing Description: This private lender is a fully capitalized nationwide bridge lender providing short to intermediate-term senior debt and mezzanine debt financing on commercial assets.
Backup Contact Name: Will Nelson, Brad Shain, Lauren Smyle
Backup Internal Notes: * Bridge/hard money across the country. * Prefer a 5 minute phone call to discuss the deal vs. an email. * No construction unless there is  pre-leasing or construction completion * $10mm - $150mm (might got to $5mm for easy deals) * Average loan is $25mm * 9% - 12% * Must hit a 13% annual yield when you take into account origination points * 80% LTC historically but will focus on the take-out (will another lender take-out to 70% LTV) * Nationwide (not a big fan of the Dakotas) * Tranched interest cost (i.e. ½ of the interest reserve needed starts collecting dutch interest day 1 and then the second ½ at a predetermined future date) * Multifamily, all the housing products, office industrial, retail, MHPs, SFR portfolio, co-living, hotels, data center, hospital (urgent center, rented by a big name tenant, not full hospital) * Cannabis - NO THC, CBD is OK (no grow facility) * No senior housing in Alabama * Land must be crossed with another income-producing properties, must be in tier 1 markets * No ground-up * Built to suit OK * No mezz due to covid * 95% of deals have some kind of recourse (can get creative) * Prepayment penalty will be structured around the exit strategy (6 months is the lowest she’s seen) * 1-3 year term (can structure it more or less) * Source of capital is one high net worth family so every decision is in-house * They don’t sell off loans, it stays on the books for the life of the loan

Program Name: First Bank of Highland Park - Stabilized
Asset Types: Apartments, Condos, 1-4 Unit Rental, Student Housing, SFR Portfolio, Medical Office, Lt Industrial, Religious
Marketing Description: This $2.25B, 65 year-old community bank, one of the 5 largest privately-held banks in Chicagoland, is headquartered in Chicago's northern suburbs, and provides full-service relationship and business banking products and CRE + C&I lending in northeastern Illinois and southeastern Wisconsin (stretching from Chicago and its western/northern suburbs into southeast Wisconsin and, selectively, into Milwaukee metro).
Backup Internal Notes: They're still into multi- and investor-resi stuff (including construction/redevelopment, but no spec land play), and, selectively, medical office/lt. industrial (owner-occ warehouse etc.), but it's a plain vanilla program and they're being very cautious at the moment -- prioritizing renewals, existing relationships, etc., versus new lending activities (and looking at higher debt yields than in the past - 9%+).  In the past they've done plenty of office and retail but right now they don't consider themselves a player for those asset types.  300 spread on 5-year Treasuries takes rates into the 8%s on 5-years, longer term than that would have to be with some sort of swap.  They only do recourse unless it's a specific HNW-type exception (e.g. get a corporate/fund guarantee that's worthwhile).

Marketing Description: This 20-year-old, independently owned and operated $3B-asset Colorado relationship bank provides full-service private and commercial banking, including real estate lending, in Colorado, Arizona, Wyoming, and Montana.
Backup Internal Notes: In this market, their focus is on core relationship-based banking (e.g. with depository relationship, etc.). They're 'out' on office and hotel (just closed a hotel development but it was a unique situation -- it was in Boulder, in entitlements for 7 years w/ very strong professional developer-guarantor team). Have a for-sale condo project going on in Winter Park but are holding off on phase 2 due to interest rate environment.  Yes on owner-occ light industrial (warehouses).  Case-by-case on land development (right of first refusal on construction loan etc.), but no on spec land purchasing.  30-year AM on multi, 25 on everything else.  Mostly 5 year terms for fixed (anything longer with swaps only), on construction/reposition stuff they can get to 3-year I/O.  Max 70% LTV on multi, 65% on industrial.  1.2x DSCR for multi, looking more at debt yield options as it takes the interest rate risk out of the equation.  Don't love small out-of-footprint sponsors as their commitment can be iffy, but when they have a good/big client they will go out-of-market to follow them (recently did a deal in TX).  In years past they'd selectively consider non- or limited recourse, but in this market it's become a non-starter "unless they put like $30mm in the bank".  Rates are relationship-priced -- floating is 325 over SOFR, fixed is in the 7s to 8s now.

Marketing Description: This nationwide USDA lender can finance most C&I and CRE projects located in “rural” areas throughout the United States. A “rural” area is defined as a city or town with a population of 50,000 or less based on the 2020 Census tract.
Internal Notes: USDA - A “rural” area is defined as a city or town with a population of 50,000 or less based on the 2020 Census tract.
Backup Internal Notes:
For a project to qualify for USDA funding, it must be located in a “rural” area, which is defined as a city or town with a population of 50,000 or less (based on the 2020 Census tract).  USDA has an “eligibility map” online where a project address can be searched to determine if it is eligible or not.  Use this link:  Eligibility (usda.gov). 

 

(Note: USDA also has a Food Supply Chain (FSC) loan program that does not require businesses to be “rural” to qualify.  If you have a project for a food manufacturer, food distributor, food processor, cold storage facility, etc. then this program would apply)

 

With the USDA programs, we can finance most Commercial & Industrial (C&I) properties, including acquisitions, ground-up construction and debt refinances.  Loan amounts at MMB range from a minimum of $1 million up to $100 million depending on the type of project. 

 

Most asset classes are eligible for USDA funding with the exception of “straight multi-family.”  USDA does have a “multi-family” loan program, but it is specific to low-income housing which MMB does not participate in.  If a project does have a residential rental or a multi-family component, it must be part of a “mixed use” property where at least 50% or more of projected revenues are from “business” or “commercial” sources.  (Think of a mixed-use property that has commercial retail on the ground level with multi-family units up above where more than 50% of revenues are generated from commercial retail.)

 

From a maximum LTC/LTV standpoint, we can finance CRE up to 85% LTV for “non-special use” properties, and up to 80% LTV for “special use” properties.

Marketing Description: Independent community bank, founded in 1921, providing full-service personal banking and residential/multifam-focused real estate lending in northeast Illinois and southeast Wisconsin.
Backup Internal Notes: 100+ year old independent community bank based in Lake County, IL, with $265mm in total assets.  They'll go as far south as northern Cook County (not as far as Chicago), and as far north into Wisconsin as Kenosha, Racine, and Walworth counties. They do a lot of 5/5 ARMs where the rate re-adjusts every 5 years but doesn't mature for 15-20+ years (fully amortizing etc.).  For true Fixed they'd go max 7-year terms (10 years ago they did 15 year fixed, fully amortizing -- not anymore).  They've done a couple of strip malls and self-storage to existing customers, they've done plenty of religious, but they mainly focus on resi/multi, both owner-occ and investor (warrantable condos, SFR portfolios, etc.).  Including construction loans/redevelopment.  For 1-4 investor product they'd go to 80% LTV -- everything else 75%.  45% DTI ratio.  They would theoretically do student housing/senior housing/assisted living, even Lt. industrial (warehouse etc.), but never really have and don't consider themselves a player in those spaces.  They'd consider going into a participation but not leading one.

Marketing Description: This nationwide USDA lender can finance most C&I and CRE projects located in “rural” areas throughout the United States. A “rural” area is defined as a city or town with a population of 50,000 or less based on the 2020 Census tract.
Internal Notes: USDA - A “rural” area is defined as a city or town with a population of 50,000 or less based on the 2020 Census tract.
Backup Internal Notes:
For a project to qualify for USDA funding, it must be located in a “rural” area, which is defined as a city or town with a population of 50,000 or less (based on the 2020 Census tract).  USDA has an “eligibility map” online where a project address can be searched to determine if it is eligible or not.  Use this link:  Eligibility (usda.gov). 

 

(Note: USDA also has a Food Supply Chain (FSC) loan program that does not require businesses to be “rural” to qualify.  If you have a project for a food manufacturer, food distributor, food processor, cold storage facility, etc. then this program would apply)

 

With the USDA programs, we can finance most Commercial & Industrial (C&I) properties, including acquisitions, ground-up construction and debt refinances.  Loan amounts at MMB range from a minimum of $1 million up to $100 million depending on the type of project. 

 

Most asset classes are eligible for USDA funding with the exception of “straight multi-family.”  USDA does have a “multi-family” loan program, but it is specific to low-income housing which MMB does not participate in.  If a project does have a residential rental or a multi-family component, it must be part of a “mixed use” property where at least 50% or more of projected revenues are from “business” or “commercial” sources.  (Think of a mixed-use property that has commercial retail on the ground level with multi-family units up above where more than 50% of revenues are generated from commercial retail.)

 

From a maximum LTC/LTV standpoint, we can finance CRE up to 85% LTV for “non-special use” properties, and up to 80% LTV for “special use” properties.

For intl' sponsors there'd have to be something tying here (e.g. US credit, addtl. co-sponsors, etc.). Couple-hour drive from Denver (all along Front Range and into the mountains).  Not as far west as Grand Junction.

Our business model is based on developing programmatic relationships with our sponsor partners, and we have invested over $55MM with one such sponsor over the last few years"
Backup Internal Notes: No California. Wants extremely strong operators that they can invest in multiple deals.
